---
title: "Ch 2 ŌĆö Inverse Trigonometric Functions"
subtitle: "Restrict the domain, gain an inverse, work the principal branch"
lesson-id: "cbse12.inverse-trig-functions"
tier: "cbse12"
ncert-class: 12
ncert-chapter: 2
ncert-chapter-title: "Inverse Trigonometric Functions"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: ["cbse11.trig-functions", "cbse12.relations-functions-types"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.differentiability-and-rules", "cbse12.integration-techniques"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Do the graphs of $\sin x$, $\cos x$, $\tan x$ feel familiar?
2. Can you read the values $\sin(\pi/6) = 1/2$, $\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2$,
   $\tan(\pi/4) = 1$ off the unit circle?
3. From last lesson ŌĆö what does it mean for a function to be **bijective**?

If no on (1) or (2), revisit
[Class 11 ┬Ę Ch 3a ŌĆö Trigonometric Functions](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/03a-trig-functions/index.qmd).
If no on (3), see
[Class 12 ┬Ę Ch 1](../01-relations-functions-types/index.qmd).
:::

## Touch

Pick any number $y$ between $-1$ and $1$. Ask: which angle $\theta$ has
$\sin \theta = y$?

Try $y = 1/2$.

::: {.lesson-beat}

| $\theta$ (degrees) | $-330┬░$ | $-210┬░$ | $30┬░$ | $150┬░$ | $390┬░$ | $510┬░$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\sin \theta$      | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ |

:::

Six different angles, all giving $\sin \theta = 1/2$. Infinitely many
in fact ŌĆö every $30┬░ + 360┬░k$ and every $150┬░ + 360┬░k$.

If you tell me $\sin \theta = 1/2$, you have **not** told me $\theta$.

So we play a game: agree once and for all that *sin's inverse will
return the angle in the range $[-90┬░, 90┬░]$ ŌĆö and only that*. Then
$\sin^{-1}(1/2) = 30┬░$, full stop.

**That trick** ŌĆö restricting the domain of $\sin$ to $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ so
it becomes bijective ŌĆö is the whole chapter.

## Wonder

1. Could you have picked a different range, say $[\pi/2, 3\pi/2]$, and
   still got a working inverse? What would it return for $\sin^{-1}(1/2)$?
2. Why must the chosen range *cover* every value of $\sin$ once?
3. The range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ contains $0$ and is symmetric ŌĆö convenient
   for what?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Find every angle $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ with $\cos \theta = -1/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Reference angle: $\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2$. The negative sign sends you to
quadrants II and III.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\theta = 2\pi/3, \ 4\pi/3$. Two answers in one period.
</details>

### Problem 2

You want to invert $\cos$. Pick a range so that *every* value in $[-1,1]$
is hit by exactly one angle. Try $[0, \pi]$. Does that work?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Sketch $\cos x$ on $[0, \pi]$. Does it hit $-1, 0, 1$ each exactly once?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos$ on $[0, \pi]$ runs strictly from $1$ down to $-1$ ŌĆö every value
in $[-1,1]$ is hit exactly once. Ō£ō This is the **principal range** for
$\cos^{-1}$.
</details>

### Problem 3

Compute $\sin^{-1}(\sin(2\pi/3))$ if $\sin^{-1}$ outputs an angle in
$[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
$\sin(2\pi/3) = \sin(\pi/3)$. Now $\pi/3$ *is* in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(2\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2 = \sin(\pi/3)$. Since $\pi/3 \in [-\pi/2,
\pi/2]$, $\sin^{-1}(\sin(2\pi/3)) = \pi/3$, **not** $2\pi/3$.

Important: $\sin^{-1}(\sin x) = x$ only on the principal branch.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The first inverse-trig tables in the world were Indian. **Aryabhata**
(499 CE), in the *Aryabhatiya*, tabulated values of *jya* (the
half-chord ŌĆö our sine) at $3.75┬░$ intervals. To navigate by stars or
to find the time from a shadow, you needed to go the *other* way:
given an observed *jya*, recover the angle. Astronomers built reverse
look-ups by hand. The Sanskrit *jya* travelled through Arabic *jiba*
into Latin *sinus* ŌĆö and the practical need to invert it (recover an
angle from a ratio) is the same need a GPS satellite has today.
:::

### Problem 4

A right triangle has legs of length 3 and 4. The hypotenuse is 5. Find
the angle $\theta$ opposite the leg of length 3.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
$\sin \theta = 3/5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\theta = \sin^{-1}(3/5) \approx 36.87┬░$. We don't write the answer
exactly because $3/5$ isn't on the standard table ŌĆö but it *is* a
unique angle, and your calculator returns it because the principal
branch makes the inverse function well-defined.
</details>

### Problem 5

Show that $\sin^{-1}(-x) = -\sin^{-1}(x)$ for $x \in [-1,1]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Use that $\sin$ is odd: $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\theta = \sin^{-1}(x)$, so $\sin \theta = x$ and $\theta \in
[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. Then $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta = -x$ and $-\theta
\in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. So $\sin^{-1}(-x) = -\theta = -\sin^{-1}(x)$. Ō£ō
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Sine isn't invertible ŌĆö *infinitely* many angles share each value. We
**chop the sine wave** down to one rising stretch, $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$,
where it becomes one-one and onto $[-1, 1]$. *That* slice has an
inverse, and we call it $\sin^{-1}$. The "principal value" is not a
property of sine; it's a *contract* we sign so the inverse exists. Every
inverse-trig identity later is just bookkeeping for the chopped piece
versus the rest of the wave.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **principal value branches** of the six inverse trig functions:

| Function | Domain | Principal range |
|:--|:-:|:-:|
| $\sin^{-1} x$ | $[-1,1]$ | $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| $\cos^{-1} x$ | $[-1,1]$ | $[0, \pi]$ |
| $\tan^{-1} x$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ |
| $\cot^{-1} x$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $(0, \pi)$ |
| $\sec^{-1} x$ | $|x| \ge 1$ | $[0, \pi] \setminus \{\pi/2\}$ |
| $\csc^{-1} x$ | $|x| \ge 1$ | $[-\pi/2, \pi/2] \setminus \{0\}$ |

Two key identities (derive from complementarity):

$$\sin^{-1} x + \cos^{-1} x = \frac{\pi}{2}, \qquad \tan^{-1} x +
\cot^{-1} x = \frac{\pi}{2}.$$

:::

A verse to remember:

> *Sine bows from $-\pi/2$ to $\pi/2$;*
> *Cosine reigns from $0$ to $\pi$, no minus side.*
> *Tangent runs the open half-line, ends excluded;*
> *Half-pi sums them ŌĆö sine with cosine, tan with cot.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the value of $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3}) + \cos^{-1}(-1/2)$.

A competent solver thinks:

1. $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3})$ ŌĆö angle in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ with $\tan = \sqrt{3}$.
   That's $\pi/3$.
2. $\cos^{-1}(-1/2)$ ŌĆö angle in $[0, \pi]$ with $\cos = -1/2$. That's
   $2\pi/3$.
3. Sum: $\pi/3 + 2\pi/3 = \pi$.

**Answer: $\pi$.**

### Practice ŌĆö Warm

**W1.** Compute $\sin^{-1}(\sqrt{3}/2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi/3$. (And not $2\pi/3$, even though $\sin(2\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$.)
</details>

**W2.** Compute $\cos^{-1}(0) + \sin^{-1}(0)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi/2 + 0 = \pi/2$. (Or: use the identity $\sin^{-1}x + \cos^{-1}x =
\pi/2$ at $x = 0$.)
</details>

**W3.** Compute $\tan^{-1}(-1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Principal range is $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. Pick the angle there with
$\tan = -1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-\pi/4$.
</details>

### Practice ŌĆö Working

**P1.** Show $\sin^{-1}(2x\sqrt{1-x^2}) = 2 \sin^{-1} x$ for
$|x| \le 1/\sqrt{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Let $\theta = \sin^{-1} x$, so $\sin\theta = x$, $\cos\theta =
\sqrt{1-x^2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = 2x\sqrt{1-x^2}$. For
$|x| \le 1/\sqrt{2}$, $|2\theta| \le \pi/2$, so $\sin^{-1}$ of both
sides gives $2\theta = 2\sin^{-1} x$. Ō£ō
</details>

**P2.** Simplify $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sin x}{1 + \cos x}\right)$ for
$x \in (-\pi, \pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Half-angle: $\sin x = 2\sin(x/2)\cos(x/2)$, $1 + \cos x = 2\cos^2(x/2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The fraction simplifies to $\tan(x/2)$. So the expression is
$\tan^{-1}(\tan(x/2)) = x/2$ (for $x/2$ in the principal branch
$(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, which holds when $x \in (-\pi, \pi)$).
</details>

**P3.** Find $\cos\big(\sin^{-1}(3/5) + \cos^{-1}(4/5)\big)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(3/5)$, so $\sin\alpha = 3/5,\ \cos\alpha = 4/5$.
Let $\beta = \cos^{-1}(4/5)$, so $\cos\beta = 4/5,\ \sin\beta = 3/5$.
$\cos(\alpha+\beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta = (4/5)
(4/5) - (3/5)(3/5) = 16/25 - 9/25 = 7/25$.
</details>

### Practice ŌĆö Stretch

**S1.** Prove $\tan^{-1} x + \tan^{-1} y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+y}{1
- xy}\right)$ when $xy < 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Use $\tan(A + B)$ formula on $\tan(\tan^{-1} x + \tan^{-1} y)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $A = \tan^{-1} x,\ B = \tan^{-1} y$, so $\tan A = x, \tan B = y$.
$\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{x+y}{1-xy}$. For $xy < 1$, $A + B \in (-\pi/2,
\pi/2)$, so $\tan^{-1}\tan(A+B) = A + B$. Ō£ō
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark MCQ #1.** $\sin^{-1}(\sin(3\pi/5)) =$ (a) $3\pi/5$ (b) $2\pi/5$
(c) $-2\pi/5$ (d) $\pi/5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3\pi/5 \notin [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. $\sin(3\pi/5) = \sin(\pi - 3\pi/5) =
\sin(2\pi/5)$. Now $2\pi/5 \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. **(b) $2\pi/5$.**
</details>

**1-mark MCQ #2.** Principal value of $\cos^{-1}(-\sqrt{3}/2)$ is
(a) $\pi/6$ (b) $5\pi/6$ (c) $-\pi/6$ (d) $11\pi/6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Need angle in $[0, \pi]$ with cosine $-\sqrt{3}/2$. **$5\pi/6$**.
</details>

**2-mark.** Find the value of $\tan^{-1}(1) + \cos^{-1}(-1/2) +
\sin^{-1}(-1/2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi/4 + 2\pi/3 + (-\pi/6) = 3\pi/12 + 8\pi/12 - 2\pi/12 = 9\pi/12 =
3\pi/4$.
</details>

**2-mark.** Show $2\tan^{-1}(1/3) + \tan^{-1}(1/7) = \pi/4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
$2\tan^{-1}(1/3) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2 \cdot 1/3}{1 - 1/9}\right)
= \tan^{-1}(3/4)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\tan^{-1}(1/3) = \tan^{-1}(3/4)$. Then $\tan^{-1}(3/4) +
\tan^{-1}(1/7) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3/4 + 1/7}{1 - 3/28}\right) =
\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{25/28}{25/28}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1) = \pi/4$. Ō£ō
</details>

**3-mark.** Express $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\cos x}{1 - \sin x}\right)$ for
$x \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ in simplest form.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Half-angle identities: $\cos x = \cos^2(x/2) - \sin^2(x/2)$,
$1 - \sin x = (\cos(x/2) - \sin(x/2))^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Numerator $= (\cos\tfrac x2 - \sin\tfrac x2)(\cos\tfrac x2 +
\sin\tfrac x2)$. Denominator $= (\cos\tfrac x2 - \sin\tfrac x2)^2$.
Fraction $= \dfrac{\cos\tfrac x2 + \sin\tfrac x2}{\cos\tfrac x2 -
\sin\tfrac x2} = \dfrac{1 + \tan(x/2)}{1 - \tan(x/2)} = \tan(\pi/4 +
x/2)$. So expression $= \pi/4 + x/2$.
</details>

**3-mark.** Find $x$ if $\sin^{-1}(1 - x) - 2\sin^{-1} x = \pi/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Move $2\sin^{-1} x$ to the right, take $\sin$ of both sides.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin^{-1}(1-x) = \pi/2 + 2\sin^{-1} x$. Take sine: $1 - x =
\sin(\pi/2 + 2\sin^{-1} x) = \cos(2\sin^{-1} x) = 1 - 2x^2$. So
$x = 2x^2 \Rightarrow x(2x - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$ or $x = 1/2$.
Check both: $x = 0$ Ō£ō, $x = 1/2$ gives $\sin^{-1}(1/2) - 2\sin^{-1}(1/2)
= -\pi/6 \ne \pi/2$, Ō£Ś. **$x = 0$.**
</details>

**5-mark long answer.** Solve for $x$:
$\tan^{-1}(2x) + \tan^{-1}(3x) = \pi/4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Use the addition formula for $\tan^{-1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 ŌĆö the technique</summary>
Take $\tan$ of both sides; check the side condition $xy < 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan^{-1}(2x) + \tan^{-1}(3x) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2x + 3x}{1 - 6
x^2}\right) = \pi/4$ (provided $6x^2 < 1$). So $\dfrac{5x}{1 - 6x^2} =
1 \Rightarrow 6x^2 + 5x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (6x - 1)(x + 1) = 0
\Rightarrow x = 1/6$ or $x = -1$.

Check the side condition: $x = 1/6 \Rightarrow 6x^2 = 1/6 < 1$ Ō£ō.
$x = -1 \Rightarrow 6x^2 = 6 > 1$ Ō£Ś ŌĆö that root came from where the
formula breaks. **$x = 1/6$.**
</details>

**6-mark Case Study.** *Camera elevation angle.*

A camera mounted on a tripod 1.5 m tall is photographing a wall 8 m
away. The wall has a painting whose bottom is 0.5 m above ground and
whose top is 3.5 m above ground.

(i) Find $\theta_1$, the angle of elevation from the camera to the
bottom of the painting. (2 marks)
(ii) Find $\theta_2$, the angle of elevation to the top. (2 marks)
(iii) The painting subtends an angle $\alpha = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ at
the camera. Compute $\tan\alpha$ exactly. (2 marks)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Set up the right triangles. Camera height 1.5, horizontal distance 8.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Bottom is 0.5 below camera, so $\tan\theta_1 = -1/8 \Rightarrow
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}(-1/8)$.

**(ii)** Top is 2 m above camera, $\tan\theta_2 = 2/8 = 1/4 \Rightarrow
\theta_2 = \tan^{-1}(1/4)$.

**(iii)** $\tan\alpha = \tan(\theta_2 - \theta_1) = \dfrac{1/4 -
(-1/8)}{1 + (1/4)(-1/8) \cdot (-1)} = \dfrac{3/8}{1 + 1/32} = \dfrac{3/8}
{33/32} = \dfrac{12}{33} = \dfrac{4}{11}$.

(Watch the sign on $\theta_1$ ŌĆö it's a *depression*, technically.)
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Ex 2.1 Q11ŌĆō14, Ex 2.2 Q1ŌĆō6.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2023 Set 2 Q5 (similar to W1);
CBSE 2022 Q19 (similar to 5-mark).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [trigonometric function](/glossary.qmd#trigonometric-function) only becomes invertible after we restrict its domain** to a stretch where it is one-one and onto its range ŌĆö that stretch is the **principal branch**.
2. **The principal ranges $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, $[0, \pi]$, $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ for $\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$ are not arbitrary** ŌĆö each is the unique stretch that contains one full pass through every output value, and the symmetry around $0$ is what makes the algebra clean.
3. **$\sin^{-1}(\sin x) = x$ holds only when $x$ is already in the principal branch**; otherwise you must replace $x$ with the angle in the principal range that has the same sine.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\sin^{-1}(\sin x) = x$ always."** Only when $x \in [-\pi/2,
   \pi/2]$. Otherwise the answer is the angle in the principal range
   that has the same sine.
2. **"$\sin^{-1} x = 1/\sin x$."** No. The $-1$ here means inverse
   *function*, not reciprocal. The reciprocal is written $\csc x$.
3. **"You can apply the addition formula $\tan^{-1} x + \tan^{-1} y =
   \tan^{-1}\frac{x+y}{1-xy}$ always."** Only when $xy < 1$. Otherwise
   the sum jumps out of $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ and you need a $\pm \pi$
   correction.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state every principal range from memory in 30 seconds?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the board-pattern items with at most one
   hint?
3. Can you spot when a problem requires the $\tan^{-1}$ addition formula
   and check $xy < 1$ before applying it?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo the 5-mark and Stretch problems.
- **0ŌĆō1 yes:** re-read *Discover* with a unit circle in front of you.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse11.trig-functions`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)
  ŌĆö values, periodicity, evenness/oddness.
- [`cbse12.relations-functions-types`](../01-relations-functions-types/index.qmd)
  ŌĆö bijectivity is what makes the inverse exist.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse12.differentiability-and-rules`](../05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)
  ŌĆö $\frac{d}{dx}\sin^{-1} x$, etc.
- [`cbse12.integration-techniques`](../07b-integration-techniques/index.qmd)
  ŌĆö many integrals land on $\sin^{-1}$ or $\tan^{-1}$.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 12, Chapter 2 ŌĆö Inverse Trigonometric Functions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 2.1ŌĆō2.2.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t3.unit-circle`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/index.qmd) and
  [`t3.trig-identities`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/index.qmd).
- Read alongside the NCERT textbook.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Recovering an angle from a 2D vector uses
`atan2(y, x)`, which is a careful real-world implementation of
$\tan^{-1}$ that handles all four quadrants. Steering, AI line-of-sight,
camera tracking ŌĆö all need it.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / engineering.** Phase recovery in signal processing, joint
angles in robotics, and the inverse kinematics of robotic arms all rest
on selecting the right principal branch.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Better Explained (the *Aha!* ŌĆö restrict the domain),
  Bh─üskara (the verse), Gelfand (problem-first sequencing).
:::
